questions.question: Define clustering in the context of machine learning and provide one simple, real-world example of its application.
keyPoints: Clustering is an unsupervised learning technique used to group unlabeled data points based on their similarities.
The goal is to partition data into clusters where points in the same cluster are more similar to each other than to points in other clusters.
Example: Customer segmentation for targeted marketing campaigns.

questions.question: Explain the core objective of the K-Means clustering algorithm and describe the two main steps involved in a single iteration of the algorithm.
keyPoints: The core objective of K-Means is to partition n data points into k clusters, minimizing the within-cluster variance (sum of squares).
Step 1 (Assignment): Each data point is assigned to the cluster whose centroid (mean) is closest to it.
Step 2 (Update): The centroids for each cluster are recalculated as the mean of all data points assigned to that cluster.

questions.question: Compare and contrast K-Means and Hierarchical clustering. Discuss their key characteristics, advantages, disadvantages, and a typical use case for each.
keyPoints: K-Means is a partitioning method that requires a pre-defined number of clusters (k), is computationally efficient for large datasets, but is sensitive to initial centroid placement and can only find spherical clusters.
Hierarchical clustering creates a tree of clusters (dendrogram) and does not require a pre-defined k; it provides a hierarchical structure but is computationally expensive for large datasets.
Advantage: K-Means is scalable. Hierarchical provides a full data hierarchy. Disadvantage: K-Means is sensitive to outliers. Hierarchical is less scalable.
Use Case: K-Means for customer segmentation. Hierarchical for phylogenetic tree construction in biology.
